elements.sent-2411-078: In this section we introduce main objects of our study such as Vassiliev invariants, chord diagrams, Jacobi diagrams and weight systems.
elements.sent-2411-080: A knot is an isotopy class of $S^1$ embeddings in $\mathbb{R ^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: A knot invariant is a function on the set of knots.
elements.sent-2411-082: Before defining Vassiliev invariants, it is necessary to expand the concept of a knot to include singular knots.
elements.sent-2411-083: A singular knot is an isotopy class of $S^1$ immersions in $\mathbb{R ^3$ such that all self-intersection points are simple double points with transversal intersections.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The continuation of knot invariants to the set of singular knots is determined by Vassiliev skein relation shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:Vass-skein .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The Vassiliev skein relation is a rule for resolving double points of a singular knot.
elements.sent-2411-086: It can be easily seen that a value of an invariant at a singular knot does not depend on an order in which its double points are resolved.
elements.sent-2411-087: The space of Vassiliev invariants of order $\leq n$ is denoted $\mathcal{V _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Vassiliev invariants form a filtered vector space, and they also form a bialgebra (see \cite[\S 4.3]{chmutov2012introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: A chord diagram of order $n$ is an oriented circle with $n$ pairs of distinct points.
elements.sent-2411-090: The set of chord diagrams of order $n$ is denoted $\mathbf{A _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Chord diagrams naturally arise in the theory of Vassiliev invariants \cite{vassiliev1990cohomology .}
elements.sent-2411-092: To demonstrate this relationship, one needs to consider $v \in \mathcal{V _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Suppose $K$ is an arbitrary singular knot with exactly $n$ double points.
elements.sent-2411-094: Next, let $K'$ be a knot that is identical to $K$ in all regions of space except for one where they differ from each other in the same way as the two diagrams in Fig.\,\ref{fig:KK' do.}
elements.sent-2411-095: We also define $K^\bullet$ to be a knot with a self-intersection in the area where $K$ and $K'$ differ, and identical to both in the remaining space (see Fig.\,\ref{fig:KK' ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: Then, $v(K) - v(K') = v(K^\bullet)$ according to Vassiliev skein relation.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, a knot $K^\bullet$ has $n + 1$ double points by construction, whereas $v$ is an invariant of order $\le n$, so, by Definition\,\ref{def:Vass_inv_def , we have $v(K^\bullet) = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Therefore, $v(K)=v(K')$ for all $v \in \mathcal{V _n$ and $K$, $K'$ are singular knots with $n$ double points that differ as shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:KK' .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: It follows that changing one of intersection points in a knot diagram does not affect a value of an invariant $v$ of a singular knot with $n$ double points.
elements.sent-2411-100: Hence, a value of $v \in \mathcal{V _n$ depends only on an order in which we meet the double points when moving along the singular knot.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Information about a relative position of double points of a singular knot can be presented in form of a chord diagram, where each double point corresponds to a chord with its endpoints being preimage (with respect to an immersion that defines a singular knot) of double points of a singular knot.
elements.sent-2411-102: A chord diagram constructed in accordance with these rules is referred to as a {\it chord diagram of a singular knot .}
elements.sent-2411-103: A chord diagram generated from a singular knot $K$ is denoted by $\sigma (K)$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Examples of this correspondence between singular knots and their chord diagrams are provided in Table\,\ref{tab:knot-chord :.}

elements.sent-2411-244: In this section, we describe a method for construction of the kernel of Lie algebra weight system and apply it to provide lowest order diagrams from $\varphi_{\mathfrak{sl _n}$ kernel.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Our method to construct the kernel of a Lie algebra weight system is based on the technique introduced by Pierre Vogel to construct a 17-order Jacobi diagram in the kernel of all Lie algebra weight systems (see \cite{vogel2011algebraic ).}
elements.sent-2411-246: The underlying idea is to multiply some Jacobi diagram by a specific element of $\Lambda$ that is killed by the corresponding character.
elements.sent-2411-247: To construct such diagram, one should remember from the previous section that elements of $\Lambda$ can be expressed as symmetric polynomials in $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-248: The Lie algebra character $\chi_L$ maps this polynomial to its value on Vogel parameters $\alpha_L$, $\beta_L$, $\gamma_L$ that can be found in Table \ref{tab:char .}

elements.section-2411-005: Relation between Chern-Simons invariants and Kontsevich integral
elements.sent-2411-287: In this section, we discuss the connection of the obtained results with the 3D quantum Chern-Simons field theory.
elements.sent-2411-288: Detailed description of the connection between the Chern-Simons theory and Vassiliev invariants can be found in particular in \cite{dunin2013kontsevich .}
elements.sent-2411-290: The answer~\eqref{WilsonLoopExpValue is very similar to the Kontsevich integral~\eqref{ki .}}
elements.sent-2411-291: The only difference is that in the Kontsevich integral instead of group factors $\mathcal{G _{n, m ^R$, there are basic elements $D_{n,m}$ of the algebra of chord diagrams.}}
elements.sent-2411-292: Actually, this is not just a similarity.
elements.sent-2411-293: In fact, Lie algebras weight systems map chord diagrams to group factors of the Wilson loop expansion~\eqref{WilsonLoopExpValue , and due to linearity of the mapping \begin{equation}\langle W_R(K)\rangle = \varphi_L^R\left(I(K)\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Thus, the results on the kernel of Lie algebras weight systems acquire the following meaning for the Chern--Simons theory.
elements.sent-2411-295: In a fixed Lie algebra $L$, there appear relations between group factors $\mathcal{G _{n, m ^R$ at a fixed order $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Therefore, not all correlators $v_{n,m (K)$ can be extracted from the Wilson loop~\eqref{WilsonLoopExpValue for the Chern--Simons theory with a fixed gauge group.}}
elements.sent-2411-297: In particular, one primitive correlator is absent already at the 8-th order in the case of $SU(N)$ gauge group\footnote{Wilson loops in the 3D Chern--Simons theory with $SU(N)$ gauge group are quantum knot invariants called colored (by a representation $R$ of $\mathfrak{sl _n$ Lie algebra) HOMFLY polynomials.}}
elements.sent-2411-298: Now let is discuss how the obtained results affect the issue of distinguishing knots with the help of colored HOMFLY polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-299: We analyze this question using a specific example.
elements.sent-2411-300: Let us consider the simplest primitive element $D_8 := \widehat{(\omega P_{sl )}\bbl$ transformed to the element of the algebra of closed Jacobi diagrams, which lies in the kernel of the $\mathfrak{sl _n$ weight system.}}
elements.sent-2411-301: Using the isomorphism between the algebra of closed Jacobi diagrams and the algebra of chord diagrams, we rewrite $D_8$ through chord diagrams using the STU relation (see Fig.~\ref{fig:STU-relation ) and obtain their definite linear combination $D'_8$.}
elements.sent-2411-302: Knowing the explicit form of $D'_8$, we construct a linear combination of singular knots using the correspondence described in Section~\ref{sec:Jacobi-diag .}
elements.sent-2411-303: Applying the Vassiliev skein relation (see Fig.~\ref{fig:Vass-skein ), from the obtained combination of singular knots, we obtain a linear combination of ordinary (not singular) knots, which we denote by $K_8$.}
elements.sent-2411-304: In addition, using the Kontsevich integral, we find the Vassiliev invariant $V_8$ of the 8-th order corresponding the chord diagram $D'_8$.
elements.sent-2411-305: Since the value of the eighth order Vassiliev invariant on a singular knot with 8 double points depends only on its chord diagram, all other Vassiliev invariants of order 8 vanish on $K_8$.
elements.sent-2411-306: Also, all Vassiliev invariants at levels below 8 vanish on $K_8$ (this follows from the definition of Vassiliev invariants).
elements.sent-2411-307: Thus, since the Vassiliev invariant $V_8$ is absent in quantum $\mathfrak{sl _n$ invariants (i.e., in colored HOMFLY polynomials), all expansion coefficients up to level 8 inclusively of any HOMFLY polynomial for a combination $K_8$ of knots is equal to 0.}
elements.sent-2411-308: How to pass from some linear combination of knots to just a difference between two knots $K - K'$? We do not know an algorithmic answer to this question.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, from a chord diagram of the $n$-th order, one can construct infinitely many singular knots with $n$ double points.
elements.sent-2411-310: Further, these singular knots can be combined with each other (added and subtracted with arbitrary coefficients).
elements.sent-2411-311: It may well happen that as a result of these manipulations, we get a linear combination of knots of the form $K - K'$ for some knots $K$ and $K'$.
elements.sent-2411-312: For small orders, this can be done manually.
elements.sent-2411-313: For example, for the second order it is easy to obtain that $V_2(4_1 + 3_1) = 0$, i.e. for the trefoil knot $3_1$ and for the figure eight knot $4_1$ absolute value of the second order Vassiliev invariant coincide.
elements.sent-2411-314: Therefore, we can imagine that there are knots for which all Vassiliev invariants coincide up to and including the 7-th order, and at the 8-th order they differ by the only one Vassiliev invariant.
elements.sent-2411-315: In this case, these knots are not distinguished by any colored HOMFLY polynomials up to and including the 8-th order (meaning the coefficients of the perturbative expansion~\eqref{WilsonLoopExpValue ).}
elements.sent-2411-316: Do such knots $K$ and $K'$ differ by Vassiliev invariants of the $9$-th order or higher? In principle, there is no reason to believe that any primitive Vassiliev invariant of order 9 or higher cannot distinguish between these two knots or any other singular knot constructed using the procedure described above.
